name: ICT1012 Operating Systems - Lab Quiz Bank (Lab1-w3 Syscall & Lab2-w5 Thread)

questions.question: In the handshake program, how many pipes are required to allow bidirectional communication between parent and child?
questions.explanation: Two pipes are needed: one for parent→child and one for child→parent, enabling full-duplex communication.

questions.question: After fork(), which end of the parent-to-child pipe (p1) should the CHILD close?
questions.explanation: The child only reads from the parent-to-child pipe, so it should close the write end p1[1] to avoid accidental writes.

questions.question: What is the correct output format when the child receives a byte in the handshake program?
questions.explanation: The spec requires the child to print "<pid>: received <x> from parent" where <pid> is the child's process ID.

questions.question: Which xv6 system call is used to get the current process ID?
questions.explanation: getpid() is the xv6 system call that returns the current process's PID, declared in user/user.h.

questions.question: After the parent writes a byte to the child and receives one back, what system call should the parent use to wait for the child to finish?
questions.explanation: wait(0) is used by the parent to wait for the child process to exit, preventing zombie processes.

questions.question: What grading command is used to test only the handshake program?
questions.explanation: The handshake task is part of the syscall lab, so ./grade-lab-syscall handshake is the correct grading command.

questions.question: In the handshake program, which end of the child-to-parent pipe (p2) should the PARENT close after fork()?
questions.explanation: The parent only reads from the child-to-parent pipe p2, so it should close the write end p2[1].

questions.question: What intentional bug is introduced in xv6 for the sniffer task?
options: memset() calls that zero newly-allocated pages are removed
pipe() does not block on read
questions.explanation: Three memset() calls (in uvmalloc and kalloc) that zero memory pages are omitted, meaning newly allocated memory retains its previous contents.

questions.question: Which system call does the sniffer program use to obtain memory that may contain the secret?
questions.explanation: sbrk() is the xv6 syscall to grow the process's heap. Because pages aren't zeroed, allocated pages may contain data from previously freed processes.

questions.question: What does the secret.c program do when it runs?
options: Writes a secret string in memory then exits (freeing the memory)
Sends a secret over a pipe
Creates a hidden file in the filesystem
questions.explanation: secret.c writes a secret string in its memory and then exits, which frees the pages. These unzeroed pages can then be allocated by sniffer.

questions.question: Why does the sniffer exploit work even though secret.c has already exited?
options: The freed pages are not zeroed before being reallocated
The secret is written to a file
xv6 shares memory between all processes
questions.explanation: Because the memset zeroing calls are removed, freed pages retain their old data. When sniffer calls sbrk(), it may receive these same pages with the secret still in them.

questions.question: Which files must be added to UPROGS in Makefile for the sniffer task?
questions.explanation: Both secret and sniffer programs must be compiled. secret writes the secret, sniffer finds it. Both need to be in UPROGS.

questions.question: What argument does the monitor system call take?
options: An integer mask whose bits specify which syscalls to monitor
A pointer to a callback function
questions.explanation: monitor(mask) takes an integer mask. Each bit corresponds to a syscall number. If the bit is set, that syscall is monitored.

questions.question: To monitor only the read system call (SYS_read = 5), what mask value should be passed to monitor()?
questions.explanation: 1 << SYS_read = 1 << 5 = 32. The mask uses bit positions, not the syscall number directly.

questions.question: What is the correct output format when a monitored syscall returns?
questions.explanation: The required format is "<pid>: syscall <name> -> <return_value>" e.g. "3: syscall read -> 1023".

questions.question: Where is the monitor_mask field added in xv6 to track monitoring state per-process?
questions.explanation: uint32 monitor_mask is added to struct proc in kernel/proc.h so each process has its own monitoring mask.

questions.question: How does the monitor mask propagate to child processes?
options: The mask is copied from parent to child in kfork(): np->monitor_mask = p->monitor_mask
Children inherit only if they call monitor() themselves
questions.explanation: In kfork() (kernel/proc.c), adding np->monitor_mask = p->monitor_mask copies the mask from the parent to the newly created child process.

questions.question: How many files must be modified/created to add a new syscall like monitor to xv6?
questions.explanation: Six files: kernel/syscall.h (number), kernel/proc.h (field), kernel/sysproc.c (implementation), kernel/syscall.c (registration), user/user.h (prototype), user/usys.pl (stub), plus Makefile = at least 6-7 changes.

questions.question: What is the value of the mask to trace ALL 31 low-bit syscalls simultaneously?
questions.explanation: 0x7FFFFFFF = 2147483647 has all 31 low bits set, tracing all defined syscalls.

questions.question: In kernel/syscall.c, where should the monitoring print statement be placed?
options: After the syscall executes and returns, inside the syscall() function
In the trap handler before syscall() is called
questions.explanation: The monitor print is added inside syscall() after p->trapframe->a0 = syscalls[num]() so it can access the return value.

questions.question: Which file contains the user-space prototype declarations for xv6 system calls?
questions.explanation: user/user.h contains prototypes like int fork(), int monitor(int), etc. that user programs include.

questions.question: What does user/usys.pl generate?
options: The actual assembly stubs in user/usys.S using RISC-V ecall instructions
questions.explanation: usys.pl is a Perl script that generates user/usys.S — assembly stubs that use the RISC-V ecall instruction to transition from user-space to kernel.

questions.question: In the uthread context switch, which register stores the address where the thread will RESUME execution?
questions.explanation: The ra (return address) register stores the memory address where the thread resumes. When ret executes, the CPU jumps to the address in ra.

questions.question: Why must the stack pointer (sp) be initialized to the END (highest address) of the stack array in thread_create?
options: The stack grows from higher addresses to lower addresses (downward)
The stack grows upward in RISC-V
The first byte of the array is reserved for the thread ID
questions.explanation: Stacks grow downward (from high to low address). The sp must start at the highest address (end of the array) so pushes decrement it into the allocated space.

questions.question: In thread_create, what value should the ra register in thread_context be set to?
options: The address of the function the thread should run (func pointer)
questions.explanation: ra must be set to func (the thread function pointer) so that when thread_switch returns with ret, the CPU jumps directly into the thread's function.

questions.question: In uthread_switch.S, what does the 'sd' (Store Doubleword) instruction do?
options: Loads a 64-bit value from memory into a register
Takes a 64-bit register value and stores it into memory
Doubles the value in a register
Signals a context switch to the OS
questions.explanation: sd (Store Doubleword) stores a 64-bit value from a CPU register into memory. E.g., sd ra, 0(a0) writes the RA register to address a0+0.

questions.question: What is the role of the 'ret' instruction at the end of thread_switch?
options: Returns to the code that called thread_switch
Jumps to the address in ra, which is the new thread's next instruction
questions.explanation: After loading the new thread's ra from its saved context, ret jumps to that address — either the start of the thread function (new thread) or where it last yielded.

questions.question: How many registers are saved/restored in the uthread context switch?
questions.explanation: The thread_context saves ra, sp, s0/fp, and s1-s11 — that's 14 registers total (the callee-saved registers per RISC-V ABI).

questions.question: What is the correct offset for storing sp in the thread_context structure (assuming ra is at offset 0)?
questions.explanation: Each uint64 is 8 bytes. ra is at offset 0, so sp is at offset 8: sd sp, 8(a0).

questions.question: Which function in uthread.c is responsible for searching for a RUNNABLE thread and triggering the context switch?
questions.explanation: thread_schedule searches the thread table for a RUNNABLE thread and calls thread_switch to execute the context switch.

questions.question: How many buckets does the hash table have in the ph (parallel hash) program?
questions.explanation: NBUCKET = 5. Keys are mapped to buckets using key % 5.

questions.question: How many total keys are used in the ph program?
questions.explanation: NKEYS = 100,000. These keys are split equally among threads for insertion.

questions.question: What is the root cause of missing keys when ph-without-locks runs with 2 threads?
options: Threads overwrite each other's linked list next pointers (race condition on put())
The hash table is too small
Thread scheduling causes some puts to be skipped
questions.explanation: Multiple threads calling put() simultaneously update the same linked list without synchronization. One thread can overwrite another's next pointer, causing keys to be lost.

questions.question: Why does the get() phase NOT require mutex locks in this implementation?
options: get() runs before all puts are done
get() runs after all puts complete AND it only reads without modifying data
The hash table is read-only by design
questions.explanation: Gets happen after all puts finish (phase separation), and reads don't modify shared state, so there's no risk of data corruption during gets.

questions.question: What is 'per-bucket locking' and why is it preferred over a single global lock?
options: It uses one lock for the whole table — safer but faster
Each bucket has its own mutex, allowing threads to work in parallel on different buckets
It locks individual keys rather than buckets
It uses read-write locks instead of mutexes
questions.explanation: Per-bucket locking assigns a separate mutex to each of the 5 buckets. Threads accessing different buckets don't block each other, enabling parallel speedup while preventing data loss.

questions.question: Which pthread call initializes a mutex lock?
questions.explanation: pthread_mutex_init(&lock, NULL) initializes a pthread mutex. The second argument is for attributes (NULL for default).

questions.question: When using per-bucket locking, where should pthread_mutex_destroy() be called?
options: In the main thread after all worker threads have finished (joined)
When the hash table is created
questions.explanation: Locks should be destroyed in main() after all threads are joined (pthread_join), ensuring no thread is still using the locks when they are destroyed.

questions.question: If 2 threads are used in the ph program, how many total get() operations are performed?
questions.explanation: Each thread iterates through all 100,000 keys during the get phase. With 2 threads, that's 2 × 100,000 = 200,000 total get operations.

questions.question: What ph_fast test checks for in the grading script?
options: 0 missing keys AND greater than 1.25x speedup with 2 threads vs 1 thread
Less than 100 missing keys with 2 threads
questions.explanation: ph_fast verifies both correctness (0 missing keys) AND performance (multi-thread speedup > 1.25x), ensuring per-bucket parallelism is actually implemented.

questions.question: Why does the ph hash table task run on a real Linux/macOS host rather than inside xv6/qemu?
options: Real Linux provides multicore hardware and pthreads for observing true parallelism and race conditions
xv6 already has a built-in hash table
questions.explanation: xv6 in qemu is often single-CPU and lacks pthreads. Real Linux with multiple cores allows threads to run truly in parallel, demonstrating actual race conditions and speedup.

questions.question: What command is used to submit lab work to Gradescope?
options: make grade then upload to Gradescope manually using the generated lab.zip from make zipball
questions.explanation: Run 'make grade' to verify all tests pass, then 'make zipball' to create lab.zip, then upload lab.zip to the Gradescope assignment.

questions.question: What xv6 shell key sequence exits the QEMU xv6 emulator?
questions.explanation: Press Ctrl+A, then release, then press X to exit the QEMU emulator running xv6.

questions.question: Why should 'make clean && make qemu' be run when testing xv6 programs?
options: Because xv6 filesystem changes persist across qemu runs; clean ensures a fresh state
make clean is only needed for the first run
questions.explanation: The xv6 filesystem image persists between qemu runs. Running make clean removes old build artifacts and ensures a fresh filesystem for testing.

questions.question: What is a race condition in the context of the hash table lab?
options: A competition between threads to finish fastest
A non-deterministic bug where output depends on the timing of thread execution
When two threads access different buckets simultaneously
A deadlock caused by improper lock ordering
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when the program's output depends on the non-deterministic scheduling of threads. In the hash table, two threads updating the same bucket can corrupt the linked list.

questions.question: Which of the following system calls are used in the handshake program? (Select ALL that apply)
questions.explanation: handshake uses pipe() to create pipes, fork() to create the child, write()/read() to send/receive bytes, getpid() for the PID in output, and wait() for the parent to wait on the child. exec() is NOT used.

questions.question: Which files must be modified or created to add a new syscall (e.g., 'monitor') to xv6? (Select ALL that apply)
questions.explanation: All 7 files need changes: syscall.h (add #define), proc.h (add field), sysproc.c (implement), syscall.c (register + names array + monitoring logic), user.h (prototype), usys.pl (entry), Makefile (UPROGS). kernel/vm.c is NOT modified for adding a syscall.

questions.question: Which registers are saved and restored during a uthread context switch? (Select ALL that apply)
questions.explanation: The callee-saved registers per RISC-V ABI are: ra, sp, s0 (fp), s1 through s11. Argument registers (a0-a7) and temp registers (t0-t6) are caller-saved and NOT preserved by thread_switch.

questions.question: Which of the following are TRUE about pipe usage in xv6? (Select ALL that apply)
options: Unused pipe ends should always be closed
A pipe created with pipe(p) gives p[0] as read end and p[1] as write end
Failing to close unused ends can cause processes to hang
A single pipe supports full-duplex (bidirectional) communication
Reading from an empty pipe with no writers open returns 0 (EOF)
questions.explanation: Options 0, 1, 2, 4 are correct. A single pipe is unidirectional (half-duplex), not full-duplex — so option 3 is FALSE. Two pipes are needed for bidirectional communication.

questions.question: Which statements about the sniffer exploit are correct? (Select ALL that apply)
options: It exploits the removal of memset() zeroing calls in uvmalloc and kalloc
sbrk() is used to allocate memory that may contain leftover data
The attack would fail if pages were properly zeroed on allocation
sniffer.c must NOT modify secret.c or the xv6 kernel
The secret persists on disk between reboots
questions.explanation: Options 0-3 are correct. The exploit relies on missing zeroing, uses sbrk(), would fail with proper zeroing, and must not modify secret.c. Option 4 is FALSE — the secret is in RAM, not on disk, and is gone after a reboot.

questions.question: Which of the following pthread calls are needed to implement per-bucket mutex locking in the hash table? (Select ALL that apply)
questions.explanation: You need to declare an array of mutexes, initialize them, lock before accessing a bucket, unlock after, and destroy when done. pthread_mutex_trylock is not required for this implementation.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describe xv6 process isolation properties? (Select ALL that apply)
options: User programs cannot directly call kernel functions
User programs cannot directly call functions in other user programs
All interactions between user programs and the kernel happen through system calls
Bugs in syscall implementation can break isolation boundaries
User programs can directly access kernel memory via pointers
questions.explanation: xv6 isolates user programs from the kernel and from each other. All kernel access is via syscalls. Kernel bugs (like the missing memset) can indeed break isolation. User programs CANNOT directly access kernel memory.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of per-bucket locking over a single global table lock? (Select ALL that apply)
options: Threads accessing different buckets can run in parallel
Eliminates all race conditions with zero performance cost
Reduces lock contention compared to a global lock
Allows higher throughput (puts/second) with multiple threads
Threads still block each other when accessing the SAME bucket
questions.explanation: Per-bucket locking allows parallelism across buckets (0), reduces contention (2), improves throughput (3), and still correctly serializes access to the same bucket (4). It does NOT eliminate ALL race conditions with zero cost — there is still some overhead (option 1 is false).
